speaker: interviewer
text: November the 28th, 1995. Survivor, Wlodka Robertson. Maiden name Blit, interviewer, Sharon Tyler. City, London. Country, England. The interview is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Sharon Tyler. Today's date is Tuesday the 28th of November, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Wlodka Robertson, and we're in London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're on again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me and spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Wlodka Robertson, and my first name is spelled W-L-O-D-K-A. And my surname is Robertson, R-O-B-E-R-T-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me your date of birth and your age now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born on the 12th of October, '31. So I'm 64.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born in Warsaw, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. If you can make your eye contact here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's me, not him. Can you tell me something about what you remember of your childhood in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember living in a quite small apartment with my parents, my twin sister, and a young Polish woman who was a kind of maid who lived in with us in this very small apartment. I remember that we-- that is, my sister and I had a room to ourselves and two beds in a small bedroom, and my parents had a sort of settee, which opened that up and the dining room. And then the kitchen was a very small kitchen, where this young woman slept on a folding bed. But I remember that we thought ourselves quite privileged. To us, it seemed quite the big apartment, because the building was, I think, mainly consisting of, sort of, single rooms, and people seemed very poor. And I remember thinking that we had quite a lot of space. And the way I remember parties. My parents had parties in the-- this room, and quite a lot of people came and went. It seemed quite a lively place.

speaker: interviewer
text: For any-- were they for any national occasion, the parties? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I don't really know, they-- because they were both very active in-- politically. And they had many friends, and they had-- perhaps they were partly meetings and partly parties. They seemed to have lots of people coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the sort of political involvement in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember that my father was very well known in that little district. And whenever we came, especially on Saturdays, he would take me and my sister for a walk, and there were all these people wanting to speak to him. And I was always upset, because we couldn't go on where we were going. And also, I remember May Day demonstrations and my father being in the front, and us being quite excited, and looking out of the window. And usually, it was-- it seemed quite a risky kind of operation. There was another police or somebody attacking them, and they were attacking back. It seemed quite a lively May Day. [LAUGHS] Sort of thing I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- because your-- your family interest was politics, was it something that was discussed at home? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I don't remember. I don't remember what was-- not that clearly in front of me and my sister. It wasn't that I understood that there was any discussion in anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents-- you know, we belonged to opposite parents, because my father was a Bundist, and my mother was a Zionist. But I don't remember this causing any great arguments. And your-- your sister, did you get on well with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were very dependent on each other. We always had just-- we had the same one toy, I remember, and always played with one doll, and one pram, and one-- one bicycle eventually between us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you compare yourself with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Yes. I think that that varies, that we used to take turns, to be described differently. But on the whole, I think that my sister is perhaps more practical and sociable, and that I was perhaps more studious and quieter. Then-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to-- tell me about your schooling that you had in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So my mother was a headmistress of a Yiddish school, and the-- the language that was Yiddish. And she-- and so my sister and I went to that school, just must've been in third-- eighth. I did that for one year, because we were-- we were seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we at that time, didn't really know Yiddish, but remember that school. And it was quite-- the children would seem very poor, the local children, in this part of Warsaw. Did you go there, because your mother was the headmistress? Or would you have gone there anyhow? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? What-- what else do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the school? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I mainly remember that there was some few children of friends of my parents whom we knew. But otherwise, I think that I felt a little out of it, in some ways, different from that, perhaps. Partly probably because my mother was the headmistress there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you might have got some sort of special treatment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in a way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or the other children thought that perhaps I did, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a Yiddish school. So presumably, the other children at the school were-- were Jewish children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was purely a Jewish school for Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was most of your social life with-- and your family's social life with other Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, completely. I can really almost not think of any friends. I-- I remember somehow my uncle's friends as being not Jewish, but not my parents' friends at all, except for this young Polish woman who lived with us and her connection, her brother, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the-- were your family religious? All your-- all your friends were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were not religious at all. I think they were probably anti-religious just because it was that sort of time, and in their reaction perhaps to the strings of the rabbis and the church. And-- but my grandfather, my mother's father, was a very religious Jew who prayed, even in the ghetto, tried to eat kosher food and then seemed very upset when-- I know it meant a lot to him, yes. And perhaps there was a slight problem between him and his daughter, because, you know, of this different way of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and did you have a lot to do with grandparents, and aunts, and uncles, and cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother's family lived probably only about less than 10 minutes walk from where we lived. And there were large families. They lived in one block of flats, my grandparents. Some were on the third floor, my aunt and her family on the bottom floor. And there was a little factory my grandfather owned and my uncle worked at. And so there was quite a family there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think, yes. We went there for Jewish holidays. I had been playing-- what's that? They're called bingo lotto there on-- on some Jewish holidays. And yes, I remember what else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my grandparents fled. There was a bath. And on Friday, there was always a carp fish swimming, waiting to be slaughtered and eaten, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that became Friday night  supper.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think yes, yes. I remember that, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there other things, sort of memories that you have, particularly from your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being taken to the park, I think. By this-- I even remember being taking in the [INAUDIBLE] at some point, but that's probably the first memory. But people-- yes, this [INAUDIBLE] was political. I think that somebody from Spain came to stay with us, some kind of refugee. And that seemed quite exciting, some young man. And he stayed, I think, for a few days, and we were being told about the Spanish Civil War. It's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had other grandparents who seemed much richer and lived in a much richer part of my parents-- my father's parents, in a not Jewish part of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also in Warsaw though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Warsaw. And we used to visit them, as well. And they lived, their daughter and the son. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they were not particularly religious. I don't remember any-- anything that they did that I would recognize as relating to their religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came from a wealthier end. And yet, your-- your father was a Bundist and socialist. Did he rebel-- was he rebelling against his--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I don't think that was quite so much of rebelling, because he was quite a leader. And he-- and-- yes. I don't know. I don't think they were particularly against his political views. By the time I knew them, I don't think they were particularly rich. He started, I think I remember, in their house, flat, rather, so bag fulls of some money, which we used to play with, which was a kind of-- I think that in the First World War, all this money suddenly became worthless, and they used to joke about it. But there were all these bag fulls of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of paper notes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And I don't think they-- but, yes. I think that they felt that they were a rather superior family, yes. I think there was that feeling, because my father-- yes. Yes, I think that in the background, that they have-- they have come from some much more established family there in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said your father was interested in politics, and there were meetings and things. Can you remember any more details, of, sort of, of what-- what the household, what your home was like in that-- with that political edge to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It seemed so normal to me just, people coming and going, and perhaps arguing in the other room about things, and drinking vodka. And-- and in the summer, we used to go on holiday, that I remember, quite often, to the mountains, where my parents did some climbing, and in the [INAUDIBLE] Tatra Mountains. But usually, there was also locally some youth camp of one of those political organizations. So they had some sort of context, and I used to get to know these young men, much younger than-- older than we were. So I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you feel is important about your childhood that you'd like to-- to say, or early childhood, at least, before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think that probably my parents were unusually busy, because they were both working and profession, very, very busy and politically, whereas I think some of the children, most of the mothers were at home and not working. So that made it perhaps a little different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who looked after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this Polish young woman from the country. I think that was quite usual, that these young women would come from just the small holdings from farms to just work in people's households. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes. I remember also how she used to take us to church. But we were told, yes, we could go to church, but shouldn't kneel. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you would go with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she would pray? Hmm. Were you aware of any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living so much in a Jewish district. And-- yes, perhaps occasionally, especially when we were on holiday, some children called us names as Jews. But so-- then the children called people different names. I think we called-- I don't think we particularly felt threatened though, offended.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you first become aware that there were difficulties, that there were problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, very soon after the Germans came, a lot of people became-- sort of-- there was some-- I don't know if they were especially hooligans, or just people around the road. There was a lot of shouting, and pushing, and so realized that we were Jewish, and therefore, people would push us-- push us and be very unpleasant. And eventually, before the ghettos started, was of gangs, I think, organized by the Germans, who'd run around, hunting for people they recognized as Jews. And, yes. By that time, I knew that it wasn't there, that I was at special risk, because I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- do you remember, though, when the Germans came into Warsaw? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't remember. There was a column of German soldiers marching. So outside this house, because by that time, I was staying with my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: How had that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- my parents left Warsaw about five days after the beginning of the war, because there was a call by the Polish government, I should imagine, to able-bodied people to leave Warsaw. They were not going to defend Warsaw. They wanted, I think, not to have it bombed. And therefore, they thought they would defend it east, Warsaw. And then suddenly, there was a kind of-- people began to walk out-- men, and sometimes women, and then some families, I think all on the same day. And I think that sort of leadership of-- of the Bund that was-- decided to go. And so my father went, and my own mother went with him. I think he maybe thought that he needed her, because he was better known, perhaps as a protection. And so we were, I and my sister, and this Polish young woman called Hella were taken to this household of my grandparents. And we stayed partly. We were left partly with the grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's, yes, who lived-- who-- and my aunt and my sister, my mother's, who had two sons. sons. So my mother and father left, and we were left on our own. But-- and then there was-- in fact, Warsaw did become defended. They somehow changed. And was bombed. I remember the bombs, and the fires, and people putting out the fires with sand and water. And then when when the Germans came in, I and my sister went to these other grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your father's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To stay with them. And then my mother came back. She smuggled her-- because by that time the Russians-- you know, there was a pact between Germans and Russians. The German-- the Russians came in into Poland and found all these Poles. And all these people were hoping to form an army against [? Warsaw. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father-- because somehow got to Vilna, which was then somewhat more independent than Russia. But my mother then somehow smuggled herself through the Russians and the German front and came back. While you were with your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And so that must have been only about six weeks after she left. So it seemed a long time to us. Because I remember she seemed quite strange in a way. She was quite a stranger when she came back. And then the Polish young woman left. Must have been back to her family or somewhere. And my mother, and my sister, and I stayed with these other grandparents. It's was a very cold winter. And they had quite a smart apartment, in a quite smart part of Warsaw. But only one room. It was kept warm. It had some fire there. And it was a very, very cold winter then.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that particularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because we had to somehow undress and dress in the same room around the fire. And my grandmother was very depressed somehow. She used to cry a lot. But my grandfather seemed a very brave man. And he was always joking and making-- so pretending it was a game of hide and seek. And so I'm guessing, because we all had to undress in the dark, in the same room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother was depressed because of the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. I believe she was already depressed, then she got more depressed. Because she-- my-- it wasn't just-- my-- my father had gone, and her other son, the younger son had also gone east. And she was very worried about them. And worried as to what was happening to us all. And so we stayed till the beginning of the ghetto, which wasn't very long. It was just a few months. Then probably about six months later--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well the ghetto started in about September 14th.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so we had to move. Yes, yes. So we had to move. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At ghetto, [INAUDIBLE] before we sort of realized that the walls were being built around this poor part of Warsaw. And-- there were some special orders that all Jews have to live there. And so my mother, and I, and my sister went to live again with the family on, that's called, Leshno Street. My grandparents were already in the part of Warsaw which was to become the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- they went somewhere else, some other part of the-- of Warsaw. In some ways, they were much more helpless than my more working class family. They-- it's how they-- unable to help themselves. But they did live some way. But we visited them less. And my aunt was a pianist. And it's satisfying to think that in the ghetto, there was rhythmic lessons for some of the children. And one other aunt was a sort of teacher of rhythmic lessons to which I and my sister went. And this aunt played. And I think that was all. I don't know how much money she got for that. But that was all. She had to somehow look after her parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the few months that you were with your father's parents before you moved into the ghetto, was your-- apart from the fact that you were all in one room, and it was very cold, was your life reasonably OK? Or-- or was it-- did it already feel very difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think, you know, we just got used to things as they happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you move to the ghetto, what do you-- what's your memory of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. That seemed almost better at first. Because we were frightened by all these gangs of Poles who used to attack us. And suddenly, you know, there were only Jews around. And we had this big-- my mother's family. And at first, it-- it seemed to me better, yes. And we played. In Warsaw there were these big kind of tenements houses with yards, courtyards, with the big gate in front. And my mother was working, was away all day. And I and my sister played in this courtyard with other children. We used to have this one bicycle which all the children shared. I remember. And yes, I remember we-- it was quite a reasonably happy time. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was with your-- so you are with your mother's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yes, my mother also had a sister-- younger sister called-- called Bella, who lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was the pianist?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was the aunt who was married to my mother's brother. It was the-- not the pianist, she was the rhythmic teacher, the dancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then there was the brother who was very resourceful. He also worked in the factory and seemed somehow to look after the family. And there was an old lady living there, rather mysterious, a sort of lodger, who was not really Jewish. But she was a cripple, and she-- she-- she wouldn't leave the ghetto. She had nowhere to go. Some mysterious-- and she was killed eventually by the Germans. But anyway, that time she was still living and teaching languages to everybody, my cousins, and French, and Russian, and German. It's a strange memory, this old woman. I don't know who she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all the families move into the same house with you? Were you all in-- your family in one room and other families in other rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Apart from this woman, we were just the family. I know that a lot of Jews were thrown out quite soon from the smaller towns around Warsaw, and thrown into Warsaw. But they didn't-- we didn't share a flat with them. No, they didn't live with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your mother working at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was-- she had been a headmistress of the school. And that school, the hall was still there. And they were running soup kitchens for children. Because there were these-- I don't know if they were charitable organizations. They got some-- I don't know where they got their money from-- [? JOINT ?] from America. I remember it being mentioned, some money came from-- through them. And there's another some sort of [? health ?] organization just called [? Tolls ?]. I think. That's-- I don't know how that worked-- how they worked. But they had-- they seemed busy, yes, with the kitchen, which she organized. And I don't know what else she was doing. Because some of it was connected to the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and your sister continued to play in the courtyard with the other children around and just busied yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the next, sort of-- the next thing that happened for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, yes, there was-- you could tell a lot of agitation, people meeting in those courtyards, and talking. And then there were placards. Which, I don't remember exactly what they said. But they must have said there was this new order, that a lot of Jews would have to be sent away from Warsaw. And always, only some would be allowed to stay if they had some special jobs. And yes, some people became very frightened. And then-- so-- and my mother had some documents at first for herself. Because she was-- I don't know connect-- I suppose they had some through the-- I know the Polish name is [POLISH] but probably Judenrat organization, I seem to remember, for herself. And me and my sister was-- I remember the first time the Germans and Ukrainians came to collect people who-- who had no documents. And I remember me slaying this piece of paper and being terribly frightened, and finding in the last minute and showing it to them. And they went way. But very soon afterwards, we sort of realized, we were told that the documents, they would not be enough. And at first we were given some sort of watches by my uncle to try to bribe the Ukrainians and Germans when they come, so that they shouldn't take us away. And then my-- it must have been my grandfather and my uncle. They built a-- it was a bunker, a hiding place, in the cellars, underneath a block of flats. Where there was a kind of secret hole through which we-- and we began to-- as soon as we could hear the Germans and the Ukrainians, and whoever they were, Latvians, and the Jewish police sometimes were coming, we would run to the cellar and block ourselves in. And we could hear the shooting outside and shouting, that whoever was found who didn't come out, would be shot. And so on. But we were not found. So yes, by that time it was certainly not a normal life. We were-- and we somehow knew very quickly that it was-- that-- that people were being killed, and that they were being gassed. Because I remember being-- he said, oh, don't worry about taking parcels of clothes or anything. In fact, I-- I was surprised after the war to discover that there were camps. I thought everyone was being killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how you'd heard that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother must have said. I think that the people-- that it was normal. That there was-- first of all, they were-- I heard about even before this happened in Warsaw, that in small towns, Jews were headed together and shot and gassed in some vans. And later on when it happened, mostly there were people running-- running away from Treblinka even. They would escape somehow and come back. Because there was nowhere for them to go, or come back to the ghetto and talk about it. I remember, one of the killer time's men talking about it, who had been in Treblinka, And-- and came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- so there was a constant threat for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew that if we were caught, you-- you-- that was, you know, that was death, really. Because then we-- what happened was that there were these shops. What was it called? In the process of factories set up by some Germans, rather like Schindler. But there was one called [? Tebbens, ?] who made some metal. I don't know. It was a metal factory. And because my grandfather and uncle had owned this little factory, and had the tools and machines, they became part of this factory. So they were kind of protected. And we just hid around them. They somehow managed to get some food. And, you know, they were allowed to be seen in their life, and move around. And we were hiding. But by that time we had moved from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Leshno, somewhere else, somewhere near that factory, which I don't even know where that was. And we were always making hiding places. And my uncle had building partitions behind rooms. Those were our hiding place. In the bathroom, somehow, which he made a partition, a tiny place. I remember once hiding there. It's really terrible, because there was no air. You couldn't really breathe. But the Germans came. And they shouted and so on, then didn't find us.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been very frightening times, those times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, certainly. You know, there-- that time being hiding and hearing just [INAUDIBLE] of these people. The Germans knew about these hiding places, tapping on walls. And it did seem that they would find us. And there was the sort of small-- just the small, not quite the uprising, when for the first time I saw someone, a friend of my mother, with a gun. We somehow left the family and was staying somewhere. I don't quite know, for a short time. And suddenly after a kind of lull, when no one was speaking, the Germans weren't coming into the ghetto. Suddenly, they were there finding people again. And this man friend of my mother's was from the [INAUDIBLE]. And he came and had a gun and led us to some hiding place. And then I think that-- then somehow when things quiet and he led us through the-- the [? Tebbens ?] place. And-- and it was-- that was really terrible, that sort of-- that particular memory. That older people who worked in the factory were told to go to some-- probably Umschlagplatz. I'm not sure where they were told to go. And they were supposed to be reselected. And then they were told that they could come back. And they sort of-- my uncle and the aunts, and so on, who were working in the factory all went. But we and my grandparents hid again in some big bunker. I don't know who again. There were lots-- there wasn't just our family, there were lots of families there and some babies crying. And I was a little bit terrified. And that seemed the sort of end. But then they came back, half my family. They were allowed to come back. But there were these terrible stories of people poisoning themselves. They told us. And some-- some families took their children. Because the children, none of which were supposed to be allowed back. Also I remember some were brought in coffins, they'd been given injections-- not-- not killed-- smuggled back. And people brought some sort of small children back. And that sort of-- [INAUDIBLE] that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: To pretend that they were dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Yes. And somehow smuggling them back. So that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: When approximately are we-- are you talking about at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think it must have been about four months before or April-- five months, perhaps. Or it was in '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just before the uprising in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- was-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then it all quieted down again. And we-- and it was then that my mother was approached by the Jewish underground, I think my father's kind of colleagues and asked-- they said that they were finding some places in-- on what's called the Aryan side, outside the ghetto, for children. And I remember Michal Klepfisz coming and talking to my mother, and how he was persuading her. She was still not sure that she should let us go. And it was then decided that, I-- she, my mother decided. She didn't ask me and my sister. That he would organize, that we would be taken out of the ghetto and he would find some Polish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Michal? Klepfisz

speaker: interviewee
text: Michal Klepfisz, he was-- he must have been about 34 at the time. He was somebody of the Bund. He was a engineer. And he was very active in the underground, the Jewish underground. And he was quite charismatic. He lived at that time on the Aryan side. He looked very Jewish, but was very sort of nonchalant. And he had friends. And people did lots for him. And he-- and he was a engineer. And he-- when we stayed with him, he was quite happy to show us how he was making these molotov bombs. We were very thrilled to see all that happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before we move on to the time when you-- when he-- found somewhere else for you to move out to, to be in hiding-- was the-- perhaps on the next tape, we can talk about how you survived in-- in the ghetto as well, and food, and things you said your mother's was working-- we can go onto that in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
